<div class="blog-item-inner">

<div class="post-meta">

May 8 2019

</div>

<div class="blog-header">

# Be Too Busy to ‘Do Coffee’

<div class="blog-subtitle">

Ruthlessly decline meetings

</div>

<div class="podcast-container style-1">

<div class="audio-player-container">

<div class="controls">

<a href="#" class="not-smooth play-pause-btn"></a>

<div class="dropdown">

<div class="dropdown-menu" aria-labelledby="podcast-open-link">

<div class="date">

May 8 2019

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="content">

**Naval:** <span style="">Another tweet was: “[You should be too busy to
‘do coffee,’ while still keeping an uncluttered
calendar.](https://twitter.com/naval/status/1002108466809323521?lang=en)”
</span>

People who know me know <span style="">I’m famous for simultaneously
doing two things. </span>

First, I keep a very clean calendar. I have almost no meetings on it.
When some people see my calendar, they almost weep.

Second<span style="">, I’m busy all the time. I’m always doing
something.</span> It’s usually work-related. It’s whatever high-impact
thing that needs to be done, that I’m most inspired to do.

The only way to do that is to constantly, and ruthlessly, decline
meetings.

<span id="more-28492569"></span>

<span style="">People want to “do coffee” and build relationships.
That’s fine early in your career, when you’re still exploring. But later
in your career</span>—<span style="">when you’re exploiting, and there
are more things coming at you than you have time
for</span>—<span style="">you have to ruthlessly cut meetings out of
your life.</span>

**Ruthlessly cut meetings**

<span style="">If someone wants a meeting, see if they will do a call
instead. If they want to call, see if they will email instead. If they
want to email, see if they will text instead. And you probably should
ignore most text messages</span>—unless they’re true
emergencies<span style="">. </span>

<span style="">You have to be utterly ruthless about dodging meetings.
When you do meetings, make them walking meetings. Do standing meetings.
Keep them short, actionable and small. </span>Nothing is getting done
i<span style="">n a meeting with eight people around a conference table.
You are literally dying one hour at a time. </span>

**Nivi:** “Doing coffee” reminds me of an old quote, I think from Steve
Jobs, when someone asked him why Apple didn’t come to a convention. His
response was something like, “Because we wouldn’t be here working.”

**Naval:** <span style="">I used to have a tough time turning people
down for meetings. Now I just tell them outright, “I don’t do
non-transactional meetings. I don’t do meetings without a strict agenda.
I don’t do meetings unless we absolutely have to.”</span>

Nivi used to do this. When people asked us for get-to-know-you meetings,
he would say, “We don’t do meetings unless it’s life-and-death urgent.”
The person has to respond, “Yeah, it’s life-and-death urgent” or there’s
no meeting.

**People will meet with you when you have proof of work**

Busy people will take your meeting when you have something important or
valuable. But you have to come with a proper calling
card.<span style=""> It should be: “Here’s what I’ve done. Here’s what I
can show you. Let’s meet if this is useful to you, and I’ll be
respectful of your time.” </span>

<span style="">You have to build up credibility. For example, when a
tech investor looks at a startup, the first thing they want to see is
evidence of product progress. They don’t just want to see a slide deck.
Product progress is the entrepreneur’s resume. </span>It’s an
unfake-able resume.

<span style="">You have to do the work. To use a crypto analogy, you
have to have proof of work. If you have that and you truly have
something interesting, then you shouldn’t hesitate to put it together in
an email and send it. Even then, when asking for a meeting, you want to
be actionable. </span>

**Free your time and mind**

I<span style="">f you think you’re going to </span>“<span style="">make
it</span>” <span style="">by networking and attending a bunch of
meetings, you’re probably wrong. Networking can be important early in
your career. And you can get serendipitous with meetings. But the odds
are pretty low. </span>

When <span style="">you meet people hoping for that lucky break, you’re
relying on [Type One luck](https://nav.al/money-luck)</span>,
<span style="">which is blind luck, and Type Two luck, which is hustle
luck. </span>

<span style="">But you’re not getting Type Three or Type Four luck,
which are the better kinds. This is where you spend time developing a
reputation and working on something. You develop a unique point of view
and are able to spot opportunities that others can’t. </span>

<span style="">A busy calendar and a busy mind will destroy your ability
to do great things in this world. If you want to do great
things</span>—<span style="">whether you’re a musician or entrepreneur
or investor</span>—<span style="">you need free time and a free
mind.</span>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>
